voiceover: You're anxious about everything AND you can't get anything done. So which one is it — ADHD or anxiety? Here's the thing: they look almost identical from the outside. Both cause avoidance. Both cause racing thoughts. Both make you stare at a task and freeze. But the reason is different. Anxiety avoids because it fears something bad will happen. ADHD avoids because the brain genuinely can't generate the activation to start. One is dread. One is a dead engine. If your avoidance comes with specific 'what ifs', lean anxiety. If it's more like blankness — no fear, just nothing — that's more ADHD. And a lot of people have both. So stop blaming your character. Your brain is working against a biology, not a personality flaw. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: ADHD or Anxiety? How to Tell the Difference #Shorts
description: ADHD and anxiety look almost identical — avoidance, racing thoughts, freezing up. But the reason behind the behavior is completely different, and knowing which one is driving you changes everything. This video breaks down the key difference in a simple, science-backed way common in ADHD and anxiety research. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of tiny rewards you actually look forward to. You pick one before a hard task. Not after. Before. So your brain has something to chase. Step one: write down five to ten things that genuinely feel good. Coffee, a playlist, a snack, ten minutes outside. Step two: before a task you keep avoiding, pick one from the list. Step three: start the task with that reward already happening. Music on, coffee in hand. You are not cheating. You are finally speaking your brain's language. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Finally Start Hard Tasks #Shorts
description: If your ADHD brain refuses to start tasks, a dopamine menu can help. This simple technique uses small rewards before a task to give your brain the dopamine boost it needs to get going. It is not a hack, it is neuroscience. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You were called 'so mature for your age' — but really, you were just exhausted from holding yourself together. Late-diagnosed ADHD often looks like a kid who tried twice as hard just to seem normal. You memorized social scripts. You hid your messy notes. You apologized constantly. Not because you were bad — but because your brain needed tools nobody gave you. The masking worked well enough to fool everyone, including you. And now as an adult, you're finally getting answers. That 'laziness' was never laziness. Sound familiar? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Signs You Grew Up Masking ADHD Without Knowing #Shorts
description: If you were always called 'mature' or 'too hard on yourself,' you might have been masking undiagnosed ADHD your whole life. Late-diagnosed ADHD is incredibly common, especially in people who learned to hide their struggles early. This is what it actually looks like — and why it was never a character flaw. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You become a different person depending on who you're with. That's Rejection Masking — changing yourself so no one has a reason to leave. It's exhausting, and it's incredibly common in ADHD. Here's what it looks like: You laugh at jokes you hate because disagreeing feels too risky. You shrink your opinions down to nothing in group chats. You say yes to plans you're dreading just to keep the peace. And alone, you can't even remember who you actually are anymore. If this is you, you're not fake — you're just wired to feel rejection really, really hard. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Rejection Masking: The ADHD Pattern You Hide #Shorts
description: Rejection Masking is a common ADHD pattern where you change who you are to avoid being disliked or rejected. It's linked to Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria and can make you feel like you've lost yourself. If this sounds like you, know that it's not a character flaw — it's how your ADHD brain is wired to protect you.

question: Which inbox makes you sweat most?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. options.result: Your brain treats every unread message as equal-priority chaos - classic ADHD overwhelm flooding.

options.label: The 1 email

options.result: One unresolved thing loops in your brain on repeat - ADHD working memory holding it hostage.

options.label: The read pile
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. options.result: You start strong then stall - ADHD task-initiation wins the open, loses the follow-through.

options.result: Context-switching between inboxes drains you equally - your ADHD brain pays a tax every single time.

introVoiceover: Four inboxes. Pick the one that makes your stomach drop the most. Your choice says something real about how your ADHD brain handles pressure - drop it in the comments.
outroVoiceover: No wrong answer here - your brain is just wired differently. Follow for daily ADHD content.
title: Which inbox stresses you out most? (ADHD test) #Shorts
description:
Pick the inbox that makes you sweat - then watch as we reveal exactly what your choice says about your ADHD brain, one option at a time. The answers are all in the video.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You felt something five minutes ago and you're still not over it. That's not being dramatic. That's ADHD. The ADHD brain has a weak emotional brake. The prefrontal cortex, the part that says 'calm down, it's fine,' is under-connected. So when a feeling fires, it fires full volume. Rejection, frustration, embarrassment, even excitement, they all arrive like emergencies. Scientists call this emotional dysregulation, and it's one of the most exhausting parts of ADHD that almost nobody talks about. Here's what helps. First, name it fast. Saying 'this is dysregulation, not reality' interrupts the spiral before it locks in. Second, change your body state before your thoughts. Cold water on your wrists, a few slow exhales, even standing up. Your nervous system responds to physical input faster than logic. Third, give yourself a ten-minute window before you react to anything that stings. The feeling is real. The emergency usually isn't. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why ADHD Feelings Hit So Much Harder #Shorts
description:
ADHD emotional dysregulation means your brain's emotional brake is weaker than most, making every feeling arrive at full volume. This isn't being dramatic, it's neuroscience, and there are real strategies that help. Learn why ADHD emotions feel so intense and what you can actually do about it.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You're anxious AND scattered. But which one came first? Here's how to tell. Anxiety usually has a specific fear attached — something bad might happen. ADHD anxiety is different. It's not dread. It's just... noise. Overwhelm with no clear source. A key difference: ADHD symptoms show up even when life is calm. Anxiety spikes around triggers. Both are real, both are exhausting, and they often travel together. So if you've been treating one and still struggling, the other might be the missing piece. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: ADHD or Anxiety? Here's How to Tell the Difference #Shorts
description: ADHD and anxiety feel similar but have key differences. If your overwhelm has no clear source and sticks around even in calm moments, that's a sign ADHD might be involved. Understanding which one is driving your symptoms is the first step to actually treating the right thing. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start a task? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of tiny pleasures you pair with hard tasks to make your brain actually want to begin. Pick your menu items first: a favorite playlist, a fancy drink, a candle, a cozy spot. Then when you sit down to work, you activate one. Not as a reward after. As fuel during. Your brain gets dopamine, the task gets done. Make your list tonight. Even three things works. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Finally Start Hard Tasks #Shorts
description:
If your ADHD brain refuses to start tasks, a dopamine menu can change everything. Learn how to pair small pleasures with hard work so your brain gets the fuel it needs to begin. This simple ADHD strategy is backed by how the dopamine system actually works.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You were called gifted. Quiet. Mature for your age. But inside you were exhausted from pretending to keep up. That's late-diagnosed ADHD. You didn't grow out of it — you just got really good at hiding it. Memorizing social scripts. Over-explaining yourself. Saying sorry constantly. Working twice as hard just to look half as functional. The masking fooled everyone, including you. And now that you know, the grief and the relief hit at the same time. That's not weakness. That's survival. Sound familiar? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Signs You Were Masking ADHD Your Whole Life #Shorts
description:
If you were called gifted or mature but always felt exhausted on the inside, you might have been masking undiagnosed ADHD for years. Late-diagnosed ADHD is incredibly common, especially in people who learned to hide their struggles to fit in. The relief and grief of finally knowing are both completely valid.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You become a completely different person depending on who you're with. That's not fake — that's Rejection Masking. It's when your brain learns to hide the real you to avoid being disliked or rejected. Here's what it looks like: You laugh at a joke you hate because disagreeing feels too risky. You shrink your whole personality at work so nobody finds you 'too much.' You go home exhausted — not from the day, but from performing a version of yourself that felt safe. Sound familiar? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Rejection Masking: Hiding Who You Are With ADHD #Shorts
description:
Rejection Masking is a common ADHD pattern where your brain learns to hide your true self to avoid being disliked or rejected. It can look like shrinking your personality, agreeing when you don't, and coming home completely drained from performing a safer version of you. If this resonates, you are not alone — this is a real, recognized ADHD experience.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

question: Which pile is living on your desk?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A wooden desk overflowing with towering stacks of papers, notebooks, and folders cascading sideways, a coffee mug perched precariously on top, sticky notes clinging to every surface, small potted succulent barely visible under the chaos.
options.result: Your brain runs on visible reminders - buried means forgotten, full stop.

options.label: The museum
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A clean minimal wooden desk with only a few perfectly placed objects - one open notebook, a single pen, a small plant, and a coaster with a mug, everything spaced deliberately with lots of open surface visible.
options.result: You hyperfocus-clean when anxious - that tidy desk hides a very loud inner storm.

options.label: The project graveyard
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. options.result: You start with full-force excitement and your desk is the evidence trail.

options.label: The floor desk
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. options.result: The desk is decorative. Your floor is your actual executive function headquarters.

introVoiceover: Pick the desk that matches yours right now - no cleaning it up first. Your choice actually says something real about how your ADHD brain processes the world. Drop your letter in the comments.
outroVoiceover: None of these are wrong - they are all just different ADHD brains doing their thing. Follow for daily ADHD content.
title: Pick your desk - what it says about your ADHD brain #Shorts
description:
Four desks, four ADHD patterns - pick the one that looks most like yours and watch what your choice reveals about how your brain actually works. The answers are in the video.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You felt a small criticism and it ruined your entire day. That is not being dramatic. That is ADHD. The ADHD brain has a weak emotional brake. The prefrontal cortex, which normally dials feelings down, is underconnected. So emotions hit full force with almost nothing to slow them. Rejection, frustration, even excitement, arrive like a wave with no shore. This is called emotional dysregulation, and it is one of the most exhausting parts of ADHD that nobody talks about. Here is what actually helps. Name it fast. Saying out loud 'this is dysregulation, not reality' activates your prefrontal cortex slightly. Then buy yourself sixty seconds. Walk away, cold water, deep breath. You are not too sensitive. Your brain is missing a dimmer switch. That is fixable with the right tools. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why ADHD Emotions Hit So Much Harder #Shorts
description: Emotional dysregulation is one of the most exhausting and least talked-about parts of ADHD. Your brain is not broken, it is missing a dimmer switch, and the science explains exactly why feelings hit twice as hard. Learn what is really happening and what actually helps in under a minute.
